questions.q: What is the worst-case time complexity of a binary search?
questions.explain: Binary search halves the search space each pass, giving O(log n) in the worst case — but it requires the list to be sorted first.

questions.q: What is the worst-case time complexity of quick sort?
questions.explain: Quick sort averages O(n log n) but degrades to O(n²) in the worst case, e.g. a poor pivot on already-sorted data.

questions.q: Which sort guarantees O(n log n) in all cases but needs O(n) extra space?
questions.explain: Merge sort is O(n log n) in best, average and worst cases but uses O(n) additional memory for merging — a classic time-vs-space trade-off.

questions.q: What is the average-case time complexity of a linear search?
questions.explain: Linear search checks each element in turn, so on average it is O(n); only the best case (target first) is O(1).

questions.q: What is the worst-case time complexity of bubble sort?
questions.explain: Bubble sort uses nested passes of adjacent comparisons, giving O(n²) worst and average case; its best case is O(n) only with a swap flag on sorted data.

questions.q: Which Big O class is described as exponential and is typical of naive recursive Fibonacci?
questions.explain: Exponential growth O(2ⁿ) means one extra item roughly doubles the time; naive recursive Fibonacci and brute-force TSP are examples.

questions.q: Simplify the complexity '3n² + 5n + 17' using Big O notation.
questions.explain: Drop constants and lower-order terms and keep the dominant term: 3n² + 5n + 17 simplifies to O(n²).

questions.q: An in-order traversal of a binary search tree outputs the values in what order?
questions.explain: In-order (Left, Node, Right) traversal of a BST visits values in ascending sorted order.

questions.q: For the tree root 50; 30 with children 20 and 40; 70 with children 60 and 80 — what is the PRE-ORDER output?
questions.explain: Pre-order visits Node, Left, Right: 50, then the left subtree (30, 20, 40), then the right subtree (70, 60, 80).

questions.q: For the same tree, what is the POST-ORDER output?
questions.explain: Post-order visits Left, Right, Node: left subtree (20, 40, 30), right subtree (60, 80, 70), then the root 50.

questions.q: A breadth-first traversal of a tree is implemented using which data structure?
questions.explain: BFS visits level by level and uses a queue (FIFO); depth-first traversals use a stack or recursion.

questions.q: What is the time complexity of push and pop operations on a stack?
questions.explain: A stack only adds or removes at the top, so push and pop are constant time O(1).

questions.q: Which statement correctly distinguishes A* from Dijkstra's algorithm?
options: A* uses only g(n), the cost so far
Dijkstra is informed and A* is uninformed
A* adds a heuristic estimate h(n) of the remaining cost, using f(n) = g(n) + h(n)
A* always explores more nodes than Dijkstra
questions.explain: A* is informed: it expands the node with the lowest f(n) = g(n) + h(n), where h(n) estimates remaining cost to the goal; Dijkstra uses g(n) only. If h(n)=0, A* reduces to Dijkstra.

questions.q: An algorithm whose best running time is only exponential, O(2ⁿ) or worse, is described as:
questions.explain: Problems solvable only in exponential time or worse are intractable; those solvable in polynomial time or better are tractable.
